nativeRange.habitat: Coral and rocky reefs, adjacent sand flats and gravel patches; adults at depths of 5–180 m; juveniles in shallow turbid coastal waters and mangrove areas

diet.notes: Generally advised to feed meaty marine foods such as chopped fish, shrimp, scallop, clam, squid, and krill. Has been reported to accept high-quality pellets and frozen formulations in some cases. Feeding once daily tends to work well; beefheart is generally not recommended.

goodWith: large peaceful fish of similar size

summary: A boldly striped juvenile that fades to reddish-pink with age, the Emperor Red Snapper is a large, fast-growing predator generally considered suitable only for very large specialist systems. Juveniles have been known to school, but adults tend to be kept alone.
careNotes:
The Emperor Red Snapper (Lutjanus sebae) is a large, fast-growing predatory snapper found across the Indo-West Pacific, from the southern Red Sea and East Africa to New Caledonia, north to southern Japan and south to Australia. Juveniles display dramatic bold red-to-black stripes on a white body; with age the striping gradually fades to an overall reddish-pink coloration.

This species has been reported to grow approximately 2.5 cm (1 inch) per month under good feeding conditions. Adult sizes vary considerably across sources: LiveAquaria cites 76 cm (30 in), while FishBase, TFH Magazine, and ReefApp all cite 116 cm (46 in) as the wild maximum; an average across sources of ~100 cm is plausible for well-fed captive specimens. Due to this wide spread, dataStatus is set to 'needs_review'.

Minimum tank volume is contested: LiveAquaria recommends 360 gallons (~1363 L), Reeflings states 500 L, ReefApp recommends 3000 L, and TFH Magazine suggests 2000 gallons (~7570 L) for a full-sized adult. A 360-gallon (1363 L) tank may be adequate for juveniles or sub-adults, but a full-grown adult is generally considered unsuitable for anything less than 3000 L.

Temperament: LiveAquaria describes this fish as 'peaceful,' but TFH Magazine, Reeflings, and ReefApp all describe adults as aggressive or semi-aggressive, particularly toward conspecifics and during feeding. Juveniles in the wild school with other Emperor Snappers but adults in captivity have been known to be belligerent toward each other and should be housed alone.

Reef safety: Not reef-safe; will eat small fish, ornamental crustaceans, and invertebrates. ReefApp lists it as 'with caution' but the majority of sources consider it incompatible with reef systems.

Care level: LiveAquaria rates this 'difficult'; TFH rates it 'moderately difficult'; Reeflings rates it 'moderate to easy.' The primary challenge is not water chemistry but the extreme tank size required and the occasional feeding refusal in newly introduced specimens. 'Advanced' has been used here to reflect the tank-size demands.

This species is known to cause ciguatera poisoning in certain Indo-Pacific regions and has significant commercial fisheries value (IUCN: Least Concern, 2015). Lifespan has been reported to reach up to 40 years in the wild (FishBase).

Filtration: Requires robust filtration, protein skimming, and regular water changes due to the high bioload of this large predator.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: Three contested values were resolved by cross-referencing FishBase, Wikipedia, and ReefApp.

1. Adult size (min): Previously set to 76 cm (LiveAquaria outlier). FishBase and Wikipedia both state that the typical size is ~60 cm (with a 116 cm wild maximum); 60 cm is now used as the minimum. The max of 116 cm is confirmed by all major sources and is unchanged.

2. Minimum tank volume: Previously 1363 L (LiveAquaria, 360 gal). ReefApp recommends 3000 L; TFH Magazine recommends ~7570 L for a full adult. The nearest available nice-values-list entry above the LiveAquaria figure that reflects the species' extreme size is 2000 L, which has been adopted. Hobbyists should treat this as an absolute floor for sub-adults; a full-grown wild-type specimen (116 cm) realistically requires 3000 L or more and is generally considered unsuitable for home aquaria.

3. Sex change: Lutjanidae are gonochoristic (fixed separate sexes). No source consulted mentions protogyny, protandry, or any form of hermaphroditism in Lutjanus sebae. Set to "none" (gonochoristic).

Bonus: juvenileColorPhase set to true — juvenile bold red-to-black striped pattern is dramatically distinct from the adult reddish-pink, documented by all sources.
breedingNotes: Broadcast spawner in the wild; captive breeding has not been documented and is considered not feasible in home aquaria given the extreme adult size and space requirements.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, native range, adult size (116 cm max), temperature range (22.9–28.5°C), and lifespan (up to 40 years). FishBase size of 116 cm is wild maximum and differs from LiveAquaria's 76 cm (30 in).

additional.name: TFH Magazine
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size of 116 cm (46 in), carnivorous diet, not reef-safe, and large tank requirements. Recommends minimum 4×16 ft tank (~2000 gal) for full adult. Reef2Reef secondary source (https://www.reef2reef.com/threads/emperor-snapper-lutjanus-sebae.55390/) returned 403 Forbidden; TFH Magazine used as replacement secondary.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (116 cm), minimum tank volume (3000 L), salinity (~1.026 SG), temperature (~26°C), temperament (aggressive), and native range. Reef safe listed as 'with caution'; majority of sources indicate 'no'.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24–28°C), pH (8.0–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), minimum tank (500 L), care level (moderate), and temperament (semi-aggressive). Size given as 35 cm captivity/35 inches wild, which appears to understate maximum size relative to other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Broadcast spawner in the wild; no documented captive breeding. Tank breeding is considered impractical due to adult size.
